behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type)
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is where the signature parsing, extraction, and validation logic operates. You can mock the LLM to return various signature patterns (correct, missing types, wrong parameters, mismatched names) and verify that the validation catches mismatches. This tests both the prompt's ability to request a specific signature format AND the validation logic. Real failures here are broken validation or a prompt that doesn't enforce the interface clearly—both caught at Integration with good mock fixtures.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM with three scenarios: (1) correct signature matching the request, (2) signature missing type hints, (3) wrong parameter names. For each, verify the service either accepts and marks valid, or rejects with a clear reason. Verify that the interface specification from the description is correctly parsed and used as the validation rule.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on first or subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: To verify that generated code actually runs and passes tests, you must execute it against real test files. This requires subprocess execution (System level)—you can't mock away Python code execution without missing failures like import errors, syntax issues, or runtime exceptions that only appear in real subprocess spawn. Additionally, you need to capture real test runner output and parse it correctly. Agentic level is equally important because the most common failure is 'LLM generated code that doesn't pass tests', but System is the infrastructure minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create three test scenarios: (1) mock LLM to return syntactically correct code that passes tests, execute via subprocess, verify pass status; (2) mock LLM to return code with a runtime error, execute via subprocess, capture the exception message and verify it's returned in the result; (3) mock LLM to return code that fails a test, execute via subprocess, verify the test failure message is captured with line numbers.

behaviors.description: Refinement step includes failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from mistakes
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the message-flow logic: does the system correctly capture test failure output and construct the next prompt with it? You can mock both the LLM (to return intentionally failing code) and the test runner (to return a specific error message), then verify the failure message appears in the next LLM invocation's prompt. This tests the orchestration logic, not whether the LLM actually learns—that's behavior 5 (Workflow). Real failures here are dropped error messages, mangled formatting, or incorrect prompt construction.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return code that will fail. Mock the test runner to return a specific failure message: 'AssertionError: expected 5, got 3 at line 12'. Trigger the refinement step. Verify that the next LLM call's prompt includes the exact failure message (not just 'test failed'). Check that the failure is presented in a format that makes sense to an LLM (e.g., 'Here is the error: <message>').

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns structured error with all attempts and test results
behaviors.justification: This is control flow and state accumulation: does the system count attempts, stop at exactly 3, and pack the results correctly? Mock the LLM to always return failing code and the test runner to always fail. Verify the service makes exactly 3 generate-test cycles, then stops and returns a structured response containing all three attempts with their test outputs. Real failures are off-by-one errors in the counter, missing results in the response, or premature stopping.
behaviors.test_description: Configure mocks so every LLM call returns code that fails every test. Trigger the service. Verify: (1) the service calls the LLM exactly 3 times (not 2, not 4), (2) the final response contains a 'failure' status, (3) the response includes all three attempts with the full test output from each, (4) no fourth attempt is made.

behaviors.description: Full loop produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong
behaviors.justification: This is the only behavior that requires multiple agentic steps in sequence, each dependent on the previous one. You must call a real LLM to generate code, actually execute that code (System), capture the real test output, feed it back to the real LLM for refinement, execute the refined code, and iterate. Each refinement depends on what the LLM produced in the prior step and what the tests returned—this is inherently a multi-step agentic workflow. Single mocks can't verify that the iteration loop actually improves the code toward passing tests.
behaviors.test_description: Select a function that requires 2–3 iterations (e.g., a sorting algorithm or tree traversal that most base LLM calls get wrong). Call the service with real LLM. Execute each generated attempt via subprocess against the actual provided tests. Verify: (1) first attempt fails tests, (2) refinement prompt includes the actual failure messages, (3) subsequent attempts improve (fewer test failures or eventually all tests pass), (4) service returns success when all tests pass, with the final code included.
